464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding pipeline**: no documented first-contact-to-first-class flow (email sequence, intake form, locker assignment, buddy system, app access setup)
- **Churn prevention & win-back**: no systematic approach to pause requests, cancellations, or inactive member re-engagement
- **Coaching quality & standards**: no audit mechanism, peer observation protocol, or performance feedback loop
- **Member health & safety incident reporting**: no escalation pathway for injuries, medical events, or safeguarding concerns
- **Data governance**: no GDPR/privacy SOP despite holding payment & health data
- **Financial processes**: no supplier ordering, invoicing, cash handling, or reconciliation SOPs documented
- **Complaints & disputes**: no formal log, response timeline, or resolution protocol
- **Referral & loyalty mechanics**: no structure for tracking, rewarding, or administering member referrals
- **Retention analytics & reporting**: no defined KPIs, dashboards, or monthly churn review cadence

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Clients (23/33)**: missing member lifecycle stages—what happens between sign-up and month 3, month 6, cancellation? Outcomes tracking absent.
- **Coaches (20/24)**: no formal development roadmap, observation/feedback SOP, or escalation for underperformance; weekly task list too vague
- **Marketing (5/5)**: likely campaign templates exist but no lead-to-member conversion SOP or nurture sequence documented
- **Class scheduling & substitution**: assume exists but no contingency for coach illness or last-minute gaps
- **Facilities**: only 5/6—likely missing deep-clean/maintenance schedule or emergency protocols

---

# **Build next**

1. **Member Onboarding (first—revenue & retention multiplier

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## REVENUE — CRITICAL PRIORITY

**→ Active Clients +18% since May (122→147), but revenue DOWN 12% month-on-month (£20,199→£17,711). AR/M collapsed to £120.48, lowest in dataset. You're signing members but pricing/upsells have evaporated.**

- **Raise standard membership price by 8–12%** immediately (currently implied £120/month average). The bigger studio justifies it; competitors charge £35–45/month, you're at ~£27 effective rate. Test £32–35.
- **Relaunch upsell menu**: PT sessions, nutrition plans, class bundles (peak times: daytime classes are ghost-empty vs evening). June churn was 0% but revenue didn't follow—members aren't upgrading.
- **Fill daytime slots aggressively**: push off-peak early-bird (6–8am) and lunch (12–1pm) classes at discounted tier membership (£18/month) to capture shift workers and retirees—high-margin add-ons.
- **Retention bonus scheme**: 6-month commitment = 1 free month. June 0% churn is gold; lock in that cohort before they leave (May was 5.74%).

---

## EXPENSES — HIGH VARIANCE

**→ Non-people costs are the outlier: £9,390 in June vs £9,233 in May. Trend Q2 (May–June–July avg ~£9,312) vs Q1 post-move (Feb–Mar–Apr avg £11,437)—stabilising post-relocation, but still 45% above Sept–Nov baseline (£4,574 avg).**

- **Audit utilities & facility rent against new studio sq footage**: bigger space should be only 1.4–1.6× cost, not 2×. Likely culprits: heating/cleaning/insurance. Renegotiate with landlord or energy supplier by July.
- **People costs (£3,686 in June) creeping up**: +14% since May (£3,550). Bigger studio needs staff, but review: are you running 2 reception staff simultaneously off-peak? Shift pattern optimisation could save £200–300/month.

---

## PROFITABILITY & TAX — STRUCTURE NOW

**→ Q2 (May–June–July: £6,850 profit est., 12% margin) lags Q1 pre-move baseline (Sept–Nov: £8,062 profit,

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **JoJo is carrying unsustainable load at 133 sessions.** Redistribute her PT caseload: move 15–20 PT clients to Eilis (who has only 11 PTs) and consider recruiting a third PT-focused coach. This frees JoJo to lead class development and mentoring instead of burnout risk.

• **Grace's 52 sessions must transfer immediately.** Assign her 17 Build: Lower classes to Eilis (opportunity to build her strength confidence outside Tuesdays), her 9 Build: Upper to JoJo, her 6 Elevate and 3 Strongevity to Eilis. Body Composition scans can be split between JoJo and Eilis as admin tasks.

• **Eilis is under-used at 48 sessions and ready to expand.** After absorbing Grace's classes above, Eilis will have solid class coverage. Use her growth headroom to shadow and lead more Build: Full Body and introduce her to Circuits to reduce JoJo's dependency there.

• **No Sweat Intros are a single point of failure.** Grace ran all 3 last month. Train both JoJo and Eilis to deliver these so new members aren't blocked if one coach is unavailable; rotate them into the timetable.

• **Build: Upper is over-dependent on JoJo (15 classes + Grace's 9).** Assign Grace's Upper classes to JoJo to consolidate her specialism and confidence, but plan to develop Eilis in this format once she's secure elsewhere.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio gaps (between PT and classes):**

1. **Add a paid "Open Studio" block on Monday and Thursday evenings** — these are your quietest days with likely dead time between sessions. Charge a lower drop-in rate to capture members already on-site for other classes who might stay for extra work, or attract new traffic during slower periods.

2. **Run a Saturday morning "Mobility + Coffee" workshop** — Mobility is underperforming (61 last month) and Saturday is your quietest day overall. Package it as a 30-min guided session followed by informal coffee; position it as recovery/prehab for weekend warriors, and charge separately to boost perceived value.

**For the struggling classes themselves:**

3. **Rebrand and reposition "Engine" and "Barbelle" with targeted invites + a trial incentive** — these two are drastically underattended (15 and 12 last month). Pick members who regularly attend "Build" classes and invite them personally to a free trial, positioning Engine/Barbelle as complementary conditioning or strength variety. Make it a 2-week challenge with a small discount if they commit to 3 sessions.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**Start with your revenue anchors, not your offerings**
List every way members currently pay you (monthly membership, classes, PT, challenges, retail, etc.). For each, nail down: actual price, what's genuinely included, what costs extra. This forces honesty about what's bloated or underpriced. Use this as your menu skeleton.

**Build two separate menus — but make them compatible**
Your coaches need the *operational truth* (what takes 30 mins vs 60, what requires certification, what kills margins). Members need the *benefit story* (transformation outcomes, social vibe, progression paths). Cross-reference them so coaches can confidently upsell without confusion or mixed messaging.

**Price-test by revealing hidden tiers**
Most gyms bury costs. Instead: clearly show a "base membership" tier, then explicitly list what "premium" and "elite" members get (priority booking, form checks, nutrition consult, guest passes). Members will self-select. This also gives your coaches a conversation framework — they're not "selling," they're matching member ambitions to the right tier.

**Make the member menu a sales conversation starter, not a brochure**
Print it pocket-sized. Coaches hand it to prospects during tours and ask: "Which of these outcomes matters most to you right now?" This turns a static price sheet into discovery. Track which tiers/services coaches ask about most — that's your next marketing focus.

**Get it live this week**
Nail the coach menu first (30 mins). Then build member menu (1-2 hours). Post it in studio and email it to your base. You'll get immediate feedback about what's confusing — that's the

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

1. **You've cracked lead volume but destroyed conversion.** July's £3 cost-per-lead looks brilliant on paper—it's 5x cheaper than June—but you've pulled in 90 leads with a 3% close rate (3 sales). April's 69 leads converted at 29%, May's 38 at 61%, June's 20 at 65%. You're attracting the wrong people now: high volume, low intent. The profit collapsed from £348 in May to £458 in July despite more leads, because you're converting almost nobody.

2. **Your targeting or creative shifted in July.** Something fundamental changed. Either you've widened your audience too far, switched to bottom-funnel creative on top-funnel audiences, or you're now reaching people outside your ideal customer profile (likely younger, less committed, browsing fitness content casually rather than seeking a gym). The denominator grew while the numerator stayed flat.

3. **You're optimising for the wrong metric.** Lead cost trending down while close rate collapses is a classic warning sign you've been A/B testing creatives or audiences that drive cheap clicks but aren't buyer-ready. Your real unit economics are: cost-per-sale. In April that was £21.75; in July it's £80.33.

**Actions**

1. **Audit your July targeting and creative immediately.** Pull the exact audiences, placements, age ranges, and creative variants you ran in July vs. June. Did you expand to "Women interested in fitness" or "Women 18–55"? Did you shift from testimonial/transformation video to generic gym equipment content? The 4.5x volume spike suggests algorithmic expansion—reign it back in. Revert to your June targeting (likely narrower, location-based, or interest-specific) and test against it.

2. **Implement lead-quality scoring before spend.** You can't afford to burn £241 on 87 low-intent leads. Add a qualification question to your lead form or post-click landing page: "Are you ready to start in the next 2 weeks?" or "Have you been to a gym before?" (or whatever filters your best converters). Use the response to pause

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REVIEW

## REVENUE — ARREST THE DECLINE & RECOVER PRICING POWER

• **Rev/member collapsed from £138 (Apr) to £109 (Jun): 21% drop in 2 months.** This is your biggest red flag. Members are churning at 12.7% in May and paying less per head. Immediate action: audit your class capacity — you've grown to 144 members but June revenue *fell* to £15,765 despite headcount up 22 from May. Classes are fuller, not more valuable. Introduce tiered pricing NOW (peak/off-peak slots at £8–12/class vs current apparent £116/month average). Test a 10% price rise on new joins; existing members on legacy rates are anchoring the average down.

• **Churn spiked to 12.7% in May after the April relocation.** New studio isn't retaining newcomers. You're acquiring (138 → 144) but losing them fast. Onboarding is weak post-move. Assign a staff member to 30-day check-in calls; offer a free PT consultation to every new member in their first month. Target: get churn back to 3–5% (your baseline Nov–Feb).

• **Last 3 months (Apr–Jun) average revenue £16,232 vs prior 3 (Jan–Mar) £14,508: +11.8% growth in headcount but only +11.9% revenue growth.** You're not monetizing scale. Introduce: (1) class bundles (10 classes at +15% margin), (2) PT bolt-on at £25/session (women's gyms have high PT uptake), (3) nutrition/supplement retail corner (40%+ margin, zero added space post-relocation).

---

## EXPENSES — CASH BURN IS STRUCTURAL, NOT APRIL'S FAULT

• **Excluding April, your net cash is flatlined: Nov £-2,751, Jan £-200, Mar £-5,307, May £3,633, Jun £-2,521.** You're burning £2–3k/month on average. Operating cash outflow is consistently £15.6–16.6k (Feb–Mar and May–Jun) but revenue is only £15–16k. Your fixed costs are at or above break-even. Post-relocation (bigger space = higher rent/utilities), this is worse. Pull your P&L: what are total monthly payroll, rent, and utilities? If combined they exceed £14k, you cannot afford 144 members at current pricing. You need
